question: does pymupdf4llm use cnns
answer: Yes, PyMuPDF4LLM utilizes a machine learning model, but it relies on a Graph Neural Network (GNN) rather than a traditional Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).Here is how the architecture and layout analysis work in this pipeline:The Layout Model: It uses a hybrid approach called PyMuPDF-Layout, which combines precise, non-machine-learning geometric heuristics with a lightweight GNN.How the GNN Works: Instead of processing page images through visual transformers or CNNs, it reads the explicit structural internals of the PDF (like font statistics, margins, and character positions). The GNN treats text boxes as nodes and their spatial relationships as edges to classify titles, paragraphs, tables, and figures.Performance: Because it evaluates the document's structure rather than full-resolution images, this GNN-based architecture runs entirely on a CPU with no GPU requirements.OCR Options: While layout analysis uses the GNN, if your PDF is scanned or image-heavy, PyMuPDF4LLM utilizes plugin-based OCR functionality (such as Tesseract, RapidOCR, or PaddleOCR) to process image text.If you tell me more about the types of PDFs you are parsing (e.g., heavily scanned papers, complex multi-column reports, or image-heavy files), I can help you fine-tune the OCR and layout parameters.

question: how to pymupdf4llm with cnns. pretty sure it supports a method , i think it's rapidocr don't remember, that uses cnns under the hood instead of expensive transformer-based solutions
answer: You are exactly right! PyMuPDF4LLM natively supports a plugin adapter for RapidOCR, which utilizes a lightweight, highly optimized CNN architecture (specifically running on onnxruntime rather than heavy transformer-based vision frameworks).Under the hood, RapidOCR relies on PaddleOCR, which uses small, ultra-fast CNN backbones (like MobileNetV3 or PP-LCNet) combined with a Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) sequence decoder. This makes it lightning-fast and capable of running smoothly on a standard CPU.Here is exactly how to set up and configure PyMuPDF4LLM to swap its default engine with the CNN-backed RapidOCR adapter:Step 1: Install the RequirementsYou need to install pymupdf4llm alongside the rapidocr-onnxruntime engine:bashpip install pymupdf4llm rapidocr-onnxruntime pillow Use o código com cuidado.Step 2: Implement the RapidOCR AdapterPyMuPDF4LLM includes a pre-made callable plugin function specifically for RapidOCR inside its ocr module. You pass this callable into the ocr_function parameter of the conversion function.pythonimport pymupdf4llm from pymupdf4llm.ocr import rapidocr_api # 1. Reference the built-in RapidOCR callable my_cnn_ocr = rapidocr_api.exec_ocr # 2. Extract markdown using RapidOCR when image text is detected md_text = pymupdf4llm.to_markdown("your_document.pdf", ocr_function=my_cnn_ocr) print(md_text) Use o código com cuidado.Advanced Control (Optional)Force CNN OCR Everywhere: By default, PyMuPDF4LLM uses Hybrid OCR, meaning it only triggers the CNN on pages (or specific regions) that lack selectable text. If you have poor-quality digital layers or corrupted text and want to force the CNN to process every single page, add force_ocr=True:pythonmd_text = pymupdf4llm.to_markdown( "corrupted_file.pdf",  ocr_function=my_cnn_ocr,force_ocr=True) Use o código com cuidado.Are your PDFs mostly in English/Chinese (which RapidOCR targets natively), or are you processing other languages? I can give you instructions on how to structure a custom wrapper if you need to feed specific language models directly to the ONNX runtime.
